_meta.note: Daemon-backed bounded snapshot of every open subscription (Phase 1, Subscriptions design section 6). Each row reports sub_id + predicate_hash (decimal string of the normalized predicate, so an agent recognizes an equivalent predicate without coupling cursors) + source_count (number of buckets the subscription currently tracks an offset for) + created_at_ms (wall clock at open) + optional last_pull_at_ms (wall clock of the last pull, omitted until the first pull). Rows are sorted by sub_id for stable pagination. The optional limit clamps the row count to MAX_SUBSCRIPTIONS (omitted = 64, also the cap); truncated is true when more subscriptions exist than were returned. limit is the only argument and it is optional, so an empty arguments object is valid.

response_example_empty._note: No open subscriptions is a valid success: subscriptions[] is empty and truncated is false. This is distinct from daemon unavailable, which is a typed error.

response_example_truncated._note: When more subscriptions are open than the clamped limit (omitted or over-cap = MAX_SUBSCRIPTIONS 64), only the first limit rows (sorted by sub_id) are returned and truncated is true. A row that has never been pulled omits last_pull_at_ms.

invariants: limit is the only argument and it is optional; an empty arguments object is valid
limit is clamped to MAX_SUBSCRIPTIONS (omitted or over-cap = 64); over-cap sets truncated
rows are sorted by sub_id for stable pagination across calls
each row carries sub_id, predicate_hash (decimal string), source_count, created_at_ms, and optional last_pull_at_ms
last_pull_at_ms is omitted for a subscription that has never been pulled
an empty subscriptions[] with truncated false is a valid success, distinct from daemon unavailable
truncated is true when more subscriptions exist than were returned
daemon unavailable is a typed daemon_unavailable error, not an empty success
